items.name: Pizza
items.blurb: The standard party rule is 3 slices per adult and 2 per child, with 8 slices in a large pizza. Round up — cold pizza disappears the next morning; a shortage is remembered forever.
items.tip: Order 60–70% of pizzas as crowd-pleasers (cheese and pepperoni) and the rest as specialty pies.

items.name: Burgers
items.blurb: Plan 1.5 quarter-pound burgers per person at a cookout. Some guests take one, plenty come back for a second — 1.5 is the number that leaves a small, safe surplus.
items.tip: Patties shrink about 25% on the grill; quarter-pound is the raw weight. Make a few patties ahead as spares rather than opening more ground beef mid-cookout.

items.name: Hot Dogs
items.blurb: Plan 1.5 hot dogs per person — 2 if hot dogs are the only main, 1 if they share the grill with burgers. The eternal problem: dogs come in packs of 10, buns in packs of 8.
items.tip: Buy to the bun math, not the dog math — leftover franks keep for weeks; leftover buns go stale in two days.

items.name: Wings
items.blurb: Plan 6 wings per person when they're an appetizer alongside other food, and 10–12 when wings are the main event. A pound of party wings is roughly 9 pieces.
items.tip: Two sauces minimum — one hot, one not. About a third of every crowd won't touch buffalo sauce.

items.name: Pulled Pork
items.blurb: Plan ⅓ lb of cooked pulled pork per person for sandwiches. Pork shoulder loses roughly half its weight to trimming, fat, and moisture — so buy twice the cooked weight in raw pork butt.
items.tip: Cook a whole extra pound or two: pulled pork freezes beautifully, and a shortage can't be fixed — shoulders take 10+ hours.

items.name: Brisket
items.blurb: Plan ½ lb of cooked brisket per person as the main. A packer brisket loses about half its raw weight to trimming and the cook, so buy raw weight equal to a pound per guest.
items.tip: If plates include two meats (brisket + something), drop to ⅓ lb cooked per person.

items.name: Tacos
items.blurb: Plan 2–3 tacos per person for a taco bar — call it 2.5. With 2 oz of seasoned meat per taco, a pound of raw ground beef makes about 8 tacos.
items.tip: A taco bar hides shortages well: when meat runs low, guests fill up on rice, beans, and toppings. Always more tortillas than you think — they double as plates.

items.name: Sandwiches
items.blurb: Plan 1.5 sandwiches per person for a lunch spread, using about 3 oz of deli meat per sandwich. For bigger groups a 6-foot party sub (feeds 20–25) is cheaper and more dramatic.
items.tip: Cut sandwiches in halves or quarters — people take smaller pieces and total consumption drops noticeably.

items.name: Fried Chicken
items.blurb: Plan 3 pieces of fried chicken per person when it's the main, 2 when it shares the table with another protein. Mixed cuts stretch further than all-breast orders.
items.tip: Ordering from a chicken chain? Their '8-piece feeds 4' math assumes sides — match their serving assumptions to yours.

items.name: Pasta
items.blurb: Plan 4 oz of dry pasta per person as a main course (a 1 lb box feeds 4) and 2 oz as a side. A 24 oz jar of sauce covers about 5 main-course servings.
items.tip: Baked pasta (ziti, lasagna) holds and serves far better at parties than sauced-to-order noodles — a full steam tray of ziti feeds ~30 as a main.

items.name: Salad
items.blurb: Plan about 2.5 oz of greens per person for a side salad — a 5 oz clamshell serves two. Dress at the last minute or serve dressing on the side.
items.tip: Heavier salads (pasta salad, potato salad) run about ½ cup per person; a gallon-size bowl serves ~25 as a side.

items.name: Appetizers
items.blurb: Before a meal, plan 6 appetizer pieces per person. If appetizers ARE the meal (cocktail party), plan 12+ pieces per person and more variety as the guest list grows.
items.tip: The reliable ratio: one cold and easy option (cheese board, veggies) per two hot passed items — cold platters absorb the rush while hot trays cycle.

items.name: Cake
items.blurb: Sheet cakes serve party-size 2×2 inch slices: about 24 from a quarter sheet, 48 from a half, 96 from a full sheet. A 9-inch round serves about 12.
items.tip: If you're also serving other desserts, a cake sized for 75% of guests never runs out.

items.name: Cupcakes
items.blurb: Plan 1.5 cupcakes per person — one each, plus seconds for the sweet-tooth half of the room. Standard batches and bakery boxes come in dozens and 24s.
items.tip: Mini cupcakes flip the math: plan 3 minis per person.

items.name: Ice
items.blurb: The catering rule is 1 lb of ice per person for drinks in cups — 2 lb per person outdoors in warm weather or when you're chilling cans and bottles in coolers.
items.tip: Chilling drinks eats far more ice than serving them: a full cooler is ⅓ drinks, ⅔ ice. Buy the extra bag; unopened ice is basically free insurance.

items.name: Soda
items.blurb: Plan 2 cans of soda per person over a 2–3 hour party (more in summer heat). A 2-liter bottle pours about six 12 oz servings and is much cheaper per ounce — but needs cups and ice.
items.tip: Skew the mix: cola and lemon-lime cover 70% of what people actually take. Diet options matter more as average guest age rises.

items.name: Beer
items.blurb: The bartender's rule: 2 drinks per person the first hour, 1 each hour after — so a 3-hour party runs about 4 beers per beer drinker. Halve the total for a mixed wine-and-beer crowd.
items.tip: Light lagers should be half of any order — it's what most guests drink most of, whatever they say they prefer.

items.name: Wine
items.blurb: A 750 ml bottle pours five glasses, and wine drinkers average about 2.5 glasses per evening — one bottle per two drinkers. A 60/40 red-to-white split fits most dinner crowds; flip it in summer.
items.tip: Unopened bottles are returnable at many shops — over-buy confidently and ask about return policy at purchase.

items.name: Coffee
items.blurb: Plan 1.5 cups per person for a morning event or dessert course. A gallon of brewed coffee pours 16 8-oz cups, and a pound of ground coffee brews about 45 cups.
items.tip: About 25–30% of coffee drinkers will ask for decaf — brew a small second urn rather than fielding the question all night.

items.name: Water
items.blurb: Plan 1.5 bottles of water per person indoors — and 3 per person for outdoor summer events, where water outdraws everything else in the cooler.
items.tip: A marker by the cooler ('write your name on your bottle') genuinely cuts abandoned half-empty bottles by half.

items.name: Chips & Dip
items.blurb: Plan 2 oz of chips per person — a 15 oz party-size bag serves 7–8 people. For dip, 3 tablespoons per person means a 16 oz container covers about 10 guests.
items.tip: Refill bowls from bags in the kitchen instead of putting out giant bowls — chips stay crisp and consumption self-regulates.
